svg.title: Network-biology design-review checklist
svg.desc: A two-column reference card with five common pitfalls in network-biology analyses on the left and the diagnostic remedy for each on the right. The five pitfalls are study bias from over-represented hub proteins, false edges in high-throughput interaction screens, tissue-aggregate networks misapplied to tissue-specific questions, the wrong gene-universe for enrichment, and dependence between overlapping pathway tests. A header summarises the move and a footer cross-references the chapter sections that introduce each failure mode.
svg.text: NETWORK BIOLOGY DESIGN REVIEW — FIVE FAILURE MODES, FIVE FIXES
Run before the analysis; re-run before trusting another paper's headline pathway list.

g.text: Hub bias.
TP53, ACTB, EGFR appear in every analysis because they are over-studied,
not because they are uniquely relevant. Reported "significance" tracks degree.
Degree-preserving null and rank against it.
Permute edges, conserve node degrees, recompute the score, report a z-score not a raw rank.
False edges.
High-throughput Y2H and AP-MS produce 30 — 50 % false-positive rates.
Predicted-only edges in STRING are noisy in poorly-studied gene families.
Filter to high-confidence experimental edges.
STRING combined score > 0.7, or restrict to BioGRID curated. Recompute under both thresholds.
Tissue mismatch.
PPI databases are species-aggregate, pooled over every cell type and condition.
Neural-specific signalling shows up as edges in the muscle PPI.
Filter edges by tissue expression of both endpoints.
GTEx or HPA expression vector for the tissue; keep edges where both proteins are expressed above threshold.
Wrong gene-universe.
ORA assumes |U| is the set of tested genes. Using "all human genes" inflates p-values;
using a custom panel without restricting U makes every panel pathway look enriched.
Set |U| to the expressed-and-tested gene set.
For RNA-seq, drop genes below the expression filter from U. For targeted panels, restrict U to the panel.
Overlapping pathways.
GO parent-and-child terms are correlated; KEGG super-pathways share genes.
A "significant" parent plus a "significant" child is one finding counted twice.
Use redundancy reduction (REVIGO, simplifyEnrichment).
Cluster significant terms by semantic similarity; report cluster representatives, not raw lists.
Cross-references: hubs and false edges → Section 22.7. Tissue mismatch and universe choice → Sections 22.3, 22.7. Overlapping pathways → Section 22.3.3.
